questions.question: When testing a **standalone** component with `TestBed.configureTestingModule(...)`, where does the component itself go?
options: In the `imports` array
In the `declarations` array
In the `providers` array
In the `bootstrap` array
questions.explanation: Standalone components/directives/pipes go in `imports`, not `declarations`. `declarations` was the NgModule-era mechanism and is not used for standalone testing.

questions.question: As of Angular v21, what is the default unit-test runner for **new** projects?
options: Vitest
Karma
Jest
Playwright
questions.explanation: Vitest is the stable default unit-test runner for new v21 projects, running in Node with DOM emulation. Karma was deprecated in 2023; Playwright is an e2e runner, not the unit-test default.

questions.question: Under the v21 zoneless default, what is the recommended way to flush signal/async updates before assertions in a `ComponentFixture` test?
options: `await fixture.whenStable()`
Call `fixture.detectChanges()` manually after every state change
Wrap assertions in `setTimeout`
Call `TestBed.flushEffects()` directly
questions.explanation: `await fixture.whenStable()` runs change detection and waits for the fixture to settle, the recommended pattern under the zoneless default. Manual `detectChanges()` is the legacy/zoneful approach and can throw `NG0100` under zoneless.

questions.question: Which API correctly sets a signal input on an OnPush/zoneless component under test so the input pipeline runs and the component is marked dirty?
options: `fixture.componentRef.setInput('name', value)`
`fixture.componentInstance.name = value`
`fixture.debugElement.setInput('name', value)`
`TestBed.setInput(Comp, 'name', value)`
questions.explanation: `fixture.componentRef.setInput('name', value)` goes through the input pipeline and marks OnPush dirty correctly. Assigning directly to `componentInstance` bypasses the input pipeline and dirty-marking.

questions.question: CDK component harnesses let you interact with components through a supported, user-facing API (e.g. `getHarness`) instead of asserting on raw DOM structure, so tests survive internal DOM refactors.
questions.explanation: True. Harnesses from `@angular/cdk/testing` drive components like a user via `getHarness`/`getAllHarnesses`, making tests resilient to DOM-structure refactors rather than coupled to brittle CSS selectors.

questions.question: In this `HttpTestingController` test, what does the final `http.verify()` call assert?
options: That no outstanding (unmatched/unflushed) HTTP requests remain
That the response body equals `{ id: 1, name: 'Ana' }`
That the request used the GET method
That `provideHttpClientTesting()` was registered correctly
questions.explanation: `verify()` asserts there are no outstanding requests left — every issued request was expected and flushed. Body and method assertions are done separately via `expect(...)` and `req.request.method`.

questions.question: Why should you avoid manually calling `fixture.detectChanges()` under the v21 zoneless default?
options: It forces a CD pass when Angular has not scheduled one and can throw `NG0100`
It is removed from the API in zoneless mode and throws a `not a function` error
It only works inside `fakeAsync` zones, which zoneless apps cannot use
It silently skips OnPush components, so assertions read stale values
questions.explanation: Under zoneless CD, manually forcing change detection when Angular has not scheduled it can throw `NG0100` (expression changed after it was checked). `detectChanges()` still exists; it is simply legacy/zoneful, so `await fixture.whenStable()` is preferred.

questions.question: The authoring APIs — `TestBed`, `ComponentFixture`, `HttpTestingController`, and CDK harnesses — are runner-agnostic and behave the same whether the spec runs under Vitest or the legacy Karma+Jasmine stack.
questions.explanation: True. The authoring APIs are runner-agnostic; the configure → create → stabilize → query/harness pipeline is identical under Vitest or Karma+Jasmine. Only the runner changes.

questions.question: What is the status of moving an **existing** Karma/Jasmine project to Vitest via `ng g @schematics/angular:refactor-jasmine-vitest`?
options: The migration schematic for existing projects is currently marked experimental, even though the new-project runner is stable
Both the new-project runner and the migration schematic are stable
Migration is impossible; existing projects must stay on Karma forever
The migration schematic is stable but the new-project Vitest runner is still experimental
questions.explanation: The new-project Vitest runner is stable in v21, but the migration schematic for existing projects is currently marked experimental — a deliberate distinction between the two.

questions.question: What makes `ng update` more than a simple package-version bump?
options: It runs migration schematics that rewrite your code (e.g. NgModule→standalone, `*ngIf`→`@if`)
It re-runs your unit tests and blocks the upgrade if any fail
It regenerates `angular.json` from scratch using the latest defaults
It compiles your app AOT and rejects the upgrade on any template type error
questions.explanation: `ng update` upgrades packages and runs migration schematics that automatically rewrite source code (NgModule→standalone, control-flow syntax, legacy decorators→signal APIs). It does not run tests or regenerate the whole workspace manifest.

questions.question: What does `ng e2e` provide out of the box?
options: A hook for a third-party runner you `ng add` (e.g. Playwright, Cypress) — Angular ships no built-in e2e runner
A built-in Protractor-based end-to-end runner that ships with the CLI
Vitest configured in browser mode for end-to-end specs
A headless Karma session that drives the full app
questions.explanation: Angular ships no built-in e2e runner; `ng e2e` is a hook for a third-party runner you `ng add` such as Playwright or Cypress. `ng test` (not `ng e2e`) is the unit runner, defaulting to Vitest in v21.

questions.question: Given this `angular.json` snippet, what happens when the initial bundle reaches 700kB?
options: The build emits a warning (over `maximumWarning`) but still succeeds (under `maximumError`)
The build fails because 700kB exceeds the warning threshold
Nothing happens; budgets only apply to component styles
The build silently tree-shakes the bundle down below 500kB
questions.explanation: 700kB is above `maximumWarning` (500kB) so a warning is emitted, but below `maximumError` (1MB) so the build still succeeds. Only crossing `maximumError` fails the build.

questions.question: Which statements about the modern `application` builder and AOT compilation (default since v17) are correct? Select all that apply.
options: AOT compiles templates to JS at build time, surfacing template type errors early
AOT ships no template compiler to the browser
The `application` builder uses esbuild for bundling and a Vite-powered dev server
AOT compiles templates in the browser at first load for faster cold start
JIT is the recommended production default; AOT is dev-only
questions.explanation: AOT compiles templates to JS at build time (early errors, no template compiler shipped to the browser), and the `application` builder is esbuild-based with a Vite dev server. AOT is explicitly not browser-time, and it is the production default — JIT is dev-only legacy.

questions.question: How is Angular's modern decorator trajectory best characterized?
options: Member decorators (`@Input`/`@Output`/`@ViewChild`) migrate to signal functions, but class decorators (`@Component`/`@Injectable`/`@Directive`/`@Pipe`) stay as the declaration mechanism
All decorators, including `@Component`, are being removed in favor of plain functions
Class decorators are deprecated, but member decorators like `@Input` remain the recommended approach
Both class and member decorators are now the standardized TC39 stage-3 form executed natively at runtime
questions.explanation: The shift is 'fewer decorators inside the class': member decorators move to `input()`/`output()`/`viewChild()`/`inject()`, while class decorators remain how you declare building blocks. Nothing replaces `@Component`/`@Injectable`/`@Directive`/`@Pipe`.

questions.question: Angular's decorators are the legacy/experimental-stage TC39 form compiled by the Angular compiler with its own metadata; they are not identical to the now-standardized TC39 decorators, and Angular relies on its compiler rather than native runtime decorators.
questions.explanation: True. Angular's decorators use its own compiler-emitted metadata and differ from the standardized TC39 decorators, so decorator behavior should be treated as Angular-defined, not stage-3 JS semantics.

questions.question: Why are signal-based member functions (`input()`, `output()`, `model()`, `viewChild()`, `inject()`) preferred over the legacy member decorators? Select all that apply.
options: They return typed signals that are reactive and work under zoneless change detection
They support required inputs, transforms, and aliases without decorator metadata
They remove `ngOnChanges`-style plumbing for reacting to input changes
They are required in order to declare a component (replacing `@Component`)
They disable AOT template type-checking to speed up builds
questions.explanation: Signal functions return typed reactive signals that work zoneless, support required inputs/transforms/aliases, and drop `ngOnChanges` plumbing. They do not replace `@Component` (a class decorator that still declares the component), and they have nothing to do with disabling AOT type-checking.

questions.question: Under the v20 style-guide refresh, how is a `UserProfile` component file and class named, and how should folders be organized?
options: File `user-profile.ts` exporting class `UserProfile` (kebab file, PascalCase class), with folders grouped by feature rather than by type
File `user-profile.component.ts` exporting class `UserProfileComponent`, with a top-level `components/` folder
File `UserProfile.ts` exporting class `userProfile`, grouped under a `services/` folder
File `user-profile.comp.ts` exporting class `UserProfileComp`, grouped by type into `pages/`
questions.explanation: The v20 style guide drops type suffixes: `user-profile.ts` (kebab file) exports class `UserProfile` (PascalCase), and the guide recommends grouping folders by feature, explicitly avoiding `components/`/`services/` type buckets.

questions.question: For configuration that must vary at **runtime**, what is recommended over compile-time `environment.ts` constants swapped via `fileReplacements`?
options: DI-provided tokens that supply the values at runtime
Hardcoding a second copy of the value in `angular.json` budgets
Inlining the value into every component template with `$localize`
A global `window` variable read directly from each service
questions.explanation: `environment.ts`/`environment.prod.ts` are swapped at build time via `fileReplacements`, so they are compile-time constants. For true runtime config, DI-provided tokens are preferred because they can supply values determined at runtime.
